sentences.zh: 邯使使見羽，欲約。

translations.literal: Zhang Han sent envoys to see Yu, wanting an agreement.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Han then sent envoys to Xiang Yu, seeking terms.

sentences.zh: 羽召軍吏謀曰：「糧少，欲聽其約。」

translations.literal: Yu summoned his officers to discuss, saying: "Provisions are few; I wish to accept his terms."
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu gathered his officers and said, "Our supplies are low. I am inclined to accept his terms."

sentences.zh: 軍吏皆曰：「善。」

translations.literal: The army officers all said: 'Good.'
translations.idiomatic: All the officers replied: 'Excellent.'

sentences.zh: 羽乃與盟洹水南殷虛上。

translations.literal: Yu then made an alliance south of the Huan River at the ruins of Yin.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu then concluded a covenant south of the Huan River at the ruins of Yin.

sentences.zh: 已盟，章邯見羽流涕，為言趙高。

translations.literal: After the covenant was made, Zhang Han saw Yu and wept, speaking to him about Zhao Gao.
translations.idiomatic: After sealing terms, Zhang Han met Xiang Yu in tears and told him what Zhao Gao had done.

sentences.zh: 羽乃立章邯為雍王，置軍中。

translations.literal: Yu then established Zhang Han as King of Yong and kept him in the army camp.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu then enfeoffed Zhang Han as King of Yong and kept him with the army.

sentences.zh: 使長史欣為上將，將秦軍行前。

translations.literal: He made Chief Clerk Xin supreme general and had him lead the Qin troops in the vanguard.
translations.idiomatic: He appointed Chief Clerk Xin as senior commander and put him at the head of the surrendered Qin forces.

sentences.zh: 至函谷關，有兵守，不得入。

translations.literal: When they reached Hangu Pass, there were troops guarding it, and they could not enter.
translations.idiomatic: At Hangu Pass they found it defended and could not get through.

sentences.zh: 聞沛公已屠咸陽，羽大怒，使當陽君擊關。

translations.literal: Hearing that the Duke of Pei had already slaughtered Xianyang, Yu was enraged and sent Lord Dangyang to attack the pass.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Yu heard the Duke of Pei had taken and sacked Xianyang, he flew into a rage and ordered Lord Dangyang to force the pass.

sentences.zh: 羽遂入，至戲西鴻門，聞沛公欲王關中，獨有秦府庫珍寶。

translations.literal: Yu then entered and reached Hong Gate west of Xi; hearing the Duke of Pei wanted to be king in Guanzhong and keep the Qin treasuries and treasures for himself.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu entered and camped at Hong Gate west of Xi, where he heard that the Duke of Pei meant to rule Guanzhong and monopolize Qin's stores and treasures.

sentences.zh: 亞父范增亦大怒，勸羽擊沛公。

translations.literal: Yafu Fan Zeng was also greatly angered and urged Yu to strike the Duke of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Zeng, Xiang Yu's "Second Father," was equally furious and urged him to attack at once.

sentences.zh: 饗士，旦日合戰。

translations.literal: He feasted the soldiers, planning to join battle the next day.
translations.idiomatic: He held a military feast and prepared to fight at dawn.

sentences.zh: 羽季父項伯素善張良。

translations.literal: Yu's younger paternal uncle Xiang Bo had long been friendly with Zhang Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu's uncle Xiang Bo was an old friend of Zhang Liang.

sentences.zh: 良時從沛公，項伯夜以語良。

translations.literal: At that time Liang followed the Duke of Pei; Xiang Bo spoke to Liang at night.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang was then serving the Duke of Pei, and Xiang Bo secretly warned him that night.

sentences.zh: 良與俱見沛公，因伯自解於羽。

translations.literal: Liang went with him to see the Duke of Pei, and through Bo explained himself to Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang went with him to see the Duke of Pei, and through Xiang Bo secured Xiang Yu's understanding.

sentences.zh: 明日，沛公從百餘騎至鴻門謝羽，自陳「封秦府庫，還軍霸上以待大王，閉關以備他盜，不敢背德。」

translations.literal: The next day, the Duke of Pei with over one hundred cavalry came to Hong Gate to apologize to Yu, personally stating: "I sealed the Qin treasuries, withdrew to Bashang awaiting the Great King, closed the pass to guard against other bandits, and dared not betray your favor."
translations.idiomatic: Next morning the Duke of Pei came to Hong Gate with a little over a hundred riders to apologize. He said he had sealed Qin's treasuries, withdrawn to Bashang to await the king, and closed the pass only to guard against other raiders, never to betray Xiang Yu.

sentences.zh: 羽意既解，范增欲害沛公，賴張良、樊噲得免。

translations.literal: Yu's intent then eased. Fan Zeng wished to kill the Duke of Pei, but thanks to Zhang Liang and Fan Kuai he escaped.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu's anger softened, though Fan Zeng still sought to kill the Duke of Pei; Zhang Liang and Fan Kuai enabled his escape.

sentences.zh: 語在高紀。

translations.literal: The account is in the Basic Annals of Gao.
translations.idiomatic: This episode is recorded in the Basic Annals of Gaozu.

sentences.zh: 後數日，羽乃屠咸陽，殺秦降王子嬰，燒其宮室，火三月不滅；

translations.literal: Several days later, Yu then slaughtered Xianyang, killed Ziying, the surrendered Qin king, burned its palaces, and the fire did not die out for three months.
translations.idiomatic: A few days later Xiang Yu sacked Xianyang, killed the surrendered Qin ruler Ziying, and burned the palaces; the fires raged for three months.

sentences.zh: 收其寶貨，略婦女而東。

translations.literal: He gathered its treasures and goods, seized women, and went east.
translations.idiomatic: He seized treasure and women and then marched east.

sentences.zh: 秦民失望。

translations.literal: The Qin people were disappointed.
translations.idiomatic: The people of Qin were left in bitter despair.

sentences.zh: 於是韓生說羽曰：「關中阻山帶河，四塞之地，肥饒，可都以伯。」

translations.literal: Then Han Sheng advised Yu, saying: "Guanzhong is blocked by mountains and girded by rivers, a land closed on all sides, fertile and rich, fit to make capital and become hegemon."
translations.idiomatic: Han Sheng advised Xiang Yu: "Guanzhong is ringed by mountains and rivers, naturally fortified and fertile. It is fit to be your capital and base of hegemony."

sentences.zh: 羽見秦宮室皆已燒殘，又懷思東歸，曰：「富貴不歸故鄉，如衣錦夜行。」

translations.literal: Yu saw that Qin palaces were all already burned and ruined, and he also longed in his heart to return east, saying: "If rich and noble and not returning to old home, it is like wearing embroidered clothes at night."
translations.idiomatic: But Xiang Yu saw the Qin palaces in ruins and yearned for the east. He said, "To gain rank and wealth yet not return home is like wearing brocade in the dark."

sentences.zh: 韓生曰：「人謂楚人沐猴而冠，果然。」

translations.literal: Han Sheng said: "People say Chu men are macaques wearing caps; indeed so."
translations.idiomatic: Han Sheng muttered, "People say Chu men are monkeys in ceremonial caps. It is true."

sentences.zh: 羽聞之，斬韓生。

translations.literal: When Yu heard this, he beheaded Han Sheng.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Yu heard it, he had Han Sheng executed.

sentences.zh: 初，懷王與諸將約，先入關者王其地。

translations.literal: At first, King Huai and the generals had agreed: whoever first entered the pass would be king there.
translations.idiomatic: Originally King Huai had sworn with his generals that whoever entered Guanzhong first would rule it.

sentences.zh: 羽既背約。

translations.literal: Yu had already violated the agreement.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu broke that covenant.

sentences.zh: 使人致命於懷王。

translations.literal: He sent someone to deliver his message to King Huai.
translations.idiomatic: He sent envoys to announce terms to King Huai.

sentences.zh: 懷王曰：「如約。」

translations.literal: King Huai said: 'As agreed.'
translations.idiomatic: King Huai replied: 'As we agreed.'

sentences.zh: 羽乃曰：「懷王者，吾家武信君所立耳，非有功伐，何以得顓主約？

translations.literal: Yu then said: "King Huai was only established by Lord Wuxin of my family; without achievements in battle, by what could he monopolize covenant decisions?
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu said, "King Huai was set up by my family's Lord Wuxin. He has no military merit, so why should he alone decide the covenant?

sentences.zh: 天下初發難，假立諸侯後以伐秦。

translations.literal: When All-under-Heaven first raised difficulty, we temporarily set up descendants of the lords to attack Qin.
translations.idiomatic: When the uprising began, we set up former royal heirs only as a temporary measure against Qin.

sentences.zh: 然身被堅執銳首事，暴露於野三年，滅秦定天下者，皆將相諸君與籍力也。

translations.literal: But those who wore armor and grasped spears to begin the enterprise, exposed in the wild for three years, and who destroyed Qin and settled the realm, were all you generals and ministers together with Ji.
translations.idiomatic: The men who donned armor, took up arms, endured exposure for three years, destroyed Qin, and pacified the realm were we commanders and ministers, together with me.

sentences.zh: 懷王亡功，固當分其地王之。」

translations.literal: King Huai has no merit; naturally we should divide lands and make kings of us."
translations.idiomatic: King Huai has no such merit. It is only right that we divide the lands and enthrone ourselves as kings."

sentences.zh: 諸將皆曰：「善。」

translations.literal: All the generals said: 'Good.'
translations.idiomatic: All the generals replied: 'Excellent.'

sentences.zh: 羽乃陽尊懷王為義帝，曰：「古之王者，地方千里，必居上游。」

translations.literal: Yu then outwardly honored King Huai as the Righteous Emperor, saying: "Kings of old, with territory of a thousand li, must dwell upstream."
translations.idiomatic: So Xiang Yu nominally elevated King Huai as "Righteous Emperor," saying, "A true king should dwell in the upper reaches."

sentences.zh: 徙之長沙，都郴。

translations.literal: He moved him to Changsha, with capital at Chen.
translations.idiomatic: He removed him to Changsha and set his seat at Chen.

sentences.zh: 乃分天下以王諸侯。

translations.literal: Then he divided the empire to enfeoff the lords.
translations.idiomatic: He then partitioned the realm and granted kingdoms to the feudal lords.

sentences.zh: 羽與范增疑沛公，業已講解，又惡背約，恐諸侯叛之，陰謀曰：「巴、蜀道險，秦之遷民皆居之。」

translations.literal: Yu and Fan Zeng suspected the Duke of Pei; though already reconciled, they also disliked breaking covenant and feared the lords would rebel, secretly plotting: "The roads of Ba and Shu are perilous, and Qin's exiles all dwell there."
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu and Fan Zeng still distrusted the Duke of Pei. Though they had reached terms, they feared openly breaking the pact might provoke revolt, so they schemed to send him to remote Ba and Shu, where Qin exiles lived amid dangerous roads.

sentences.zh: 乃曰：「巴、蜀亦關中地。」

translations.literal: Then they said: "Ba and Shu are also Guanzhong land."
translations.idiomatic: They claimed, "Ba and Shu count as part of Guanzhong too."

sentences.zh: 故立沛公為漢王，王巴、蜀、漢中。

translations.literal: Therefore they established the Duke of Pei as King of Han, ruling Ba, Shu, and Hanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: So they made the Duke of Pei King of Han, assigning him Ba, Shu, and Hanzhong.

sentences.zh: 而參分關中，王秦降將以距塞漢道。

translations.literal: And they split Guanzhong into three parts, making kings of surrendered Qin generals to block the Han roads through the passes.
translations.idiomatic: They also divided Guanzhong into three kingdoms under surrendered Qin generals to block Han's route through the passes.

sentences.zh: 乃立章邯為雍王，王咸陽以西。

translations.literal: They then established Zhang Han as King of Yong, ruling west of Xianyang.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Han was made King of Yong, with lands west of Xianyang.

sentences.zh: 長史司馬欣，故櫟陽獄吏，嘗有德於梁；

translations.literal: Chief Clerk Sima Xin, formerly a prison officer of Liyang, had once shown favor to Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Sima Xin, once a Liyang jail officer, had formerly shown kindness to Xiang Liang.

sentences.zh: 都尉董翳，本勸章邯降。

translations.literal: Commandant Dong Yi had originally urged Zhang Han to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Yi had originally persuaded Zhang Han to capitulate.

sentences.zh: 故立欣為塞王，王咸陽以東至河；

translations.literal: Therefore they established Xin as King of Sai, ruling east of Xianyang to the River.
translations.idiomatic: So Sima Xin was made King of Sai, with territory east of Xianyang up to the Yellow River.

sentences.zh: 立翳為翟王，王上郡。

translations.literal: They established Yi as King of Di, ruling Shang Commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Yi was made King of Di, ruling Shang Commandery.

sentences.zh: 徙魏王豹為西魏王，王河東。

translations.literal: They moved King Bao of Wei to be King of Western Wei, ruling Hedong.
translations.idiomatic: Wei king Bao was relocated as King of Western Wei over Hedong.

sentences.zh: 瑕丘公申陽者，張耳嬖臣也，先下河南，迎楚河上。

translations.literal: Lord Shenyang of Xiaqiu was a favored minister of Zhang Er; he had first pacified Henan and welcomed Chu at the River.
translations.idiomatic: Shenyang of Xiaqiu, a favorite of Zhang Er, had taken Henan early and welcomed Chu forces at the river.

sentences.zh: 立陽為河南王。

translations.literal: They established Yang as King of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: He was enfeoffed as King of Henan.

sentences.zh: 趙將司馬卬定河內，數有功。

translations.literal: Zhao general Sima Ang pacified Henei and had several merits.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao general Sima Ang had repeatedly earned merit in pacifying Henei.

sentences.zh: 立卬為殷王，王河內。

translations.literal: They established Ang as King of Yin, ruling Henei.
translations.idiomatic: He was made King of Yin over Henei.

sentences.zh: 徙趙王歇王代。

translations.literal: They moved King Xie of Zhao to rule Dai.
translations.idiomatic: King Xie of Zhao was moved to Dai.

sentences.zh: 趙相張耳素賢，又從入關，立為常山王，王趙地。

translations.literal: Zhao chancellor Zhang Er had long been esteemed and had also followed into the pass, so he was established as King of Changshan, ruling Zhao lands.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Er, Zhao's respected chancellor, had followed the western campaign, so he was made King of Changshan over Zhao territory.

sentences.zh: 當陽君英布為楚將，常冠軍。

translations.literal: Lord Dangyang Ying Bu was a Chu general and often won championship honors.
translations.idiomatic: Ying Bu, Lord Dangyang, was a leading Chu commander and often took top honors.

sentences.zh: 立布為九江王。

translations.literal: They established Bu as King of Jiujiang.
translations.idiomatic: He was made King of Jiujiang.

sentences.zh: 番君吳芮帥百粵佐諸侯從入關。

translations.literal: Lord Wu Rui of Fan led the Baiyue to assist the lords and followed into the pass.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Rui, lord of Fan, had led Baiyue forces to aid the coalition into Guanzhong.

sentences.zh: 立芮為衡山王。

translations.literal: They established Rui as King of Hengshan.
translations.idiomatic: He was made King of Hengshan.

sentences.zh: 義帝柱國共敖將兵擊南郡，功多，因立為臨江王。

translations.literal: Gong Ao, Pillar of State under the Righteous Emperor, led troops attacking Nan Commandery with great merit, and so was established as King of Linjiang.
translations.idiomatic: Gong Ao, pillar of the Righteous Emperor, had won major merit in the campaign against Nan Commandery, so he was made King of Linjiang.

sentences.zh: 徙燕王韓廣為遼東王。

translations.literal: Moved King Guang of Yan to become King of Liaodong.
translations.idiomatic: They relocated King Guang of Yan to become King of Liaodong.

sentences.zh: 燕將臧荼從楚救趙，因從入關。

translations.literal: Yan general Zang Tu had followed Chu in rescuing Zhao and then followed into the pass.
translations.idiomatic: Yan commander Zang Tu had joined Chu in rescuing Zhao and then entered the pass with the coalition.

sentences.zh: 立荼為燕王。

translations.literal: They established Tu as King of Yan.
translations.idiomatic: He was made King of Yan.

sentences.zh: 徙齊王田市為膠東王。

translations.literal: Moved King Shi of Qi to become King of Jiaodong.
translations.idiomatic: They relocated King Shi of Qi to become King of Jiaodong.

sentences.zh: 齊將田都從共救趙，入關。

translations.literal: Qi general Tian Du had followed in rescuing Zhao and entered the pass.
translations.idiomatic: Qi general Tian Du had joined the rescue of Zhao and entered Guanzhong.

sentences.zh: 立都為齊王。

translations.literal: They established Du as King of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: He was made King of Qi.

sentences.zh: 故秦所滅齊王建孫田安，羽方渡河救趙，安下濟北數城，引兵降羽。

translations.literal: Tian An, grandson of Tian Jian the king of Qi destroyed by Qin, when Yu had just crossed the River to rescue Zhao, had taken several Jibei cities and led troops to surrender to Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Tian An, grandson of Qi's last pre-Qin king Tian Jian, had seized several Jibei cities and surrendered to Xiang Yu when he crossed the river to aid Zhao.

sentences.zh: 立安為濟北王。

translations.literal: They established An as King of Jibei.
translations.idiomatic: He was made King of Jibei.

sentences.zh: 田榮者，背梁不肯助楚擊秦，以故不得封。

translations.literal: Tian Rong had betrayed Liang and refused to aid Chu against Qin; therefore he received no enfeoffment.
translations.idiomatic: Because Tian Rong had defied Xiang Liang and refused to aid Chu against Qin, he was denied a kingdom.

sentences.zh: 陳餘棄將印去，不從入關，然素聞其賢，有功於趙，聞其在南皮，故因環封之三縣。

translations.literal: Chen Yu abandoned his general's seal and left, not following into the pass; yet he had long been reputed worthy and had merit for Zhao. Hearing he was at Nanpi, they therefore by way of encirclement enfeoffed him with three counties.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Yu had cast aside his command seal and not entered the pass, but his reputation and service to Zhao were well known; hearing he was at Nanpi, Xiang Yu gave him three counties as a token grant.

sentences.zh: 番君將梅鋗功多，故封十萬戶侯。

translations.literal: Lord of Fan's general Mei Dun achieved much merit, therefore enfeoffed as marquis of one hundred thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: The Lord of Fan's general Mei Dun had achieved great merit. Therefore he was enfeoffed as a marquis with one hundred thousand households.

sentences.zh: 羽自立為西楚伯王，王梁楚地九郡，都彭城。

translations.literal: Yu established himself as Hegemon-King of Western Chu, ruling nine commanderies of Liang and Chu, with capital at Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu proclaimed himself Hegemon-King of Western Chu over nine commanderies of Liang-Chu, with his capital at Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 諸侯各就國。

translations.literal: The feudal lords each went to their states.
translations.idiomatic: The feudal lords each departed for their own domains.

sentences.zh: 田榮聞羽徙齊王市膠東，而立田都為齊王，大怒，不肯遣市之膠東，因以齊反，迎擊都。

translations.literal: When Tian Rong heard Yu had moved Qi king Shi to Jiaodong and established Tian Du as king of Qi, he was furious, refused to send Shi to Jiaodong, and with Qi rebelled, attacking Du.
translations.idiomatic: When Tian Rong learned Xiang Yu had moved King Shi of Qi to Jiaodong and installed Tian Du, he raged, refused to dispatch Shi east, and rebelled in Qi against Tian Du.

sentences.zh: 都走楚。

translations.literal: Du fled to Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Du fled to Chu.

sentences.zh: 市畏羽，乃亡之膠東就國。

translations.literal: Shi feared Yu, and so fled to Jiaodong to take up his state.
translations.idiomatic: King Shi, fearing Xiang Yu, fled to Jiaodong to assume his post.

sentences.zh: 榮怒，追殺之即墨，自立為齊王。

translations.literal: Rong grew angry, pursued and killed him at Jimo, and established himself king of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Rong, still enraged, pursued and killed him at Jimo, then declared himself king of Qi.

sentences.zh: 予彭越將軍印，今反梁地。

translations.literal: [I] granted General Peng Yue a general's seal; now he rebels in Liang lands.
translations.idiomatic: He granted Peng Yue a general's seal, and Peng Yue now rose in rebellion in Liang territory.

sentences.zh: 越乃擊殺濟北王田安。

translations.literal: Yue then attacked and killed Tian An, king of Jibei.
translations.idiomatic: Peng Yue then attacked and killed Tian An, king of Jibei.

sentences.zh: 田榮遂并王三齊之地。

translations.literal: Tian Rong then combined and ruled the lands of the three Qis.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Rong thus united and ruled all three Qi regions.

sentences.zh: 時漢王還定三秦。

translations.literal: At that time the King of Han had returned and pacified the Three Qins.
translations.idiomatic: At that time the Han king had returned and secured the Three Qin kingdoms.

sentences.zh: 羽聞漢并關中，且東，齊、梁畔之，大怒，乃以故吳令鄭昌為韓王以距漢，令蕭公角等擊彭越。

translations.literal: When Yu heard Han had annexed Guanzhong and was about to move east, while Qi and Liang had rebelled, he was greatly enraged; therefore he made Zheng Chang, former Wu magistrate, king of Han to block Han, and ordered Xiao Gongjiao and others to attack Peng Yue.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Yu learned Han had taken Guanzhong and was moving east while Qi and Liang were in revolt, he furiously installed Zheng Chang as a rival "Han king" to block Liu Bang and sent Xiao Gongjiao and others against Peng Yue.

sentences.zh: 越敗蕭公角等。

translations.literal: Yue defeated Xiao Gongjiao and others.
translations.idiomatic: Peng Yue defeated Xiao Gongjiao and the other commanders.

sentences.zh: 時，張良徇韓，遺項王書曰：「漢王失職，欲得關中，如約即止，不敢東。」

translations.literal: At the time, Zhang Liang was campaigning in Han and sent Xiang king a letter saying: "The King of Han has lost his position and only wants Guanzhong; if according to covenant, then he will stop and dare not go east."
translations.idiomatic: At that time Zhang Liang was rallying Han territory and sent Xiang Yu a letter: "The Han king merely seeks Guanzhong in line with the agreement; once granted, he will halt and not move east."

sentences.zh: 又以齊、梁反書遺羽，羽以此故無西意，而北擊齊。

translations.literal: He also sent to Yu letters that Qi and Liang had rebelled. Because of this Yu had no intention west, and instead struck Qi in the north.
translations.idiomatic: He also sent reports of the Qi and Liang rebellions, so Xiang Yu abandoned any western move and turned north to attack Qi.

sentences.zh: 徵兵九江王布。

translations.literal: Conscripted troops from Jiujiang King Bu.
translations.idiomatic: He conscripted troops from Ying Bu, King of Jiujiang.

sentences.zh: 布稱疾不行，使將將數千人往。

translations.literal: Bu claimed illness and did not go, sending a general with several thousand men instead.
translations.idiomatic: Ying Bu feigned illness and did not come in person, sending a subordinate with several thousand men.

sentences.zh: 二年，羽陰使九江王布殺義帝。

translations.literal: In the second year, Yu secretly had Jiujiang king Bu kill the Righteous Emperor.
translations.idiomatic: In the second year, Xiang Yu secretly ordered Ying Bu, king of Jiujiang, to assassinate the Righteous Emperor.

sentences.zh: 陳餘使張同、夏說說齊王榮，曰：「項王為天下宰不平，今盡王故王於醜地，而王群臣諸將善地，逐其故主趙王，乃北居代，餘以為不可。

translations.literal: Chen Yu sent Zhang Tong and Xia Shuo to persuade king Rong of Qi, saying: "Xiang king as steward of All-under-Heaven is unfair. Now he makes all former kings rule ugly lands, while giving his ministers and generals good lands; he drove out the former Zhao king to dwell in northern Dai. I think this cannot stand.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Yu sent Zhang Tong and Xia Shuo to persuade Tian Rong of Qi: "Xiang Yu has apportioned the realm unjustly, exiling rightful kings to barren lands while rewarding his own followers with rich ones; he even expelled Zhao's former king to far northern Dai. This is intolerable.

sentences.zh: 聞大王起兵，且不聽不義，願大王資餘兵，使擊常山，以復趙王，請以國為扞蔽。」

translations.literal: Hearing great king has raised troops and will not submit to unrighteousness, I wish the great king to provide me troops to strike Changshan and restore the Zhao king; I ask to make our state your defensive screen."
translations.idiomatic: I hear you have risen against this injustice. Lend me troops to attack Changshan and restore Zhao's king, and Zhao will stand as your shield state."

sentences.zh: 齊王許之，因遣兵往。

translations.literal: The king of Qi consented and therefore sent troops.
translations.idiomatic: The Qi king agreed and sent forces.

sentences.zh: 陳餘悉三縣兵，與齊併力擊常山，大破之。

translations.literal: Chen Yu gathered all troops of the three counties and, combining strength with Qi, attacked Changshan and greatly defeated it.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Yu mobilized all troops from his three counties and, joining Qi, struck Changshan and won a great victory.

sentences.zh: 張耳走歸漢。

translations.literal: Zhang Er fled and returned to Han.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Er fled back to Han.

sentences.zh: 陳餘迎故趙王歇反之趙。

translations.literal: Chen Yu welcomed back former Zhao king Xie to return to Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Yu brought the former Zhao king Xie back to Zhao.

sentences.zh: 趙王因立餘為代王。

translations.literal: The Zhao king then established Yu as king of Dai.
translations.idiomatic: In return, the Zhao king made Chen Yu king of Dai.

sentences.zh: 羽至城陽，田榮亦將兵會戰。

translations.literal: When Yu reached Chengyang, Tian Rong also led troops to meet in battle.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Yu reached Chengyang, Tian Rong came out with his army to engage him.

sentences.zh: 榮不勝，走至平原，平原民殺之。

translations.literal: Rong was defeated and fled to Pingyuan, where Pingyuan people killed him.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Rong lost, fled to Pingyuan, and was killed there by local people.

sentences.zh: 羽遂北燒夷齊城郭室屋，皆阬降卒，係虜老弱婦女。

translations.literal: Yu then northward burned and destroyed Qi's city walls and houses, all buried surrendered troops alive, and bound as captives old and weak and women.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu then devastated northern Qi, burning cities and homes, burying surrendered soldiers alive, and enslaving the old, the weak, and women.

sentences.zh: 徇齊至北海，所過殘滅。

translations.literal: He campaigned through Qi up to Beihai; wherever he passed he destroyed and wiped out.
translations.idiomatic: He swept through Qi as far as Beihai, destroying everything in his path.

sentences.zh: 齊人相聚而畔之。

translations.literal: The people of Qi gathered and rebelled against him.
translations.idiomatic: The people of Qi rallied and rebelled again.

sentences.zh: 於是田榮弟橫收得亡卒數萬人，反城陽。

translations.literal: Thereupon Tian Rong's younger brother Heng gathered tens of thousands of scattered survivors and turned back to Chengyang.
translations.idiomatic: Then Tian Heng, Tian Rong's younger brother, gathered tens of thousands of fugitives and counterattacked at Chengyang.

sentences.zh: 羽因留，連戰未能下。

translations.literal: Yu therefore stayed, fighting repeated battles and still could not take it down.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu was forced to remain there, and despite repeated fighting he could not subdue it.

sentences.zh: 漢王劫五諸侯兵，凡五十六萬人，東伐楚。

translations.literal: The King of Han gathered troops from five lords, in all five hundred sixty thousand men, and campaigned east against Chu.
translations.idiomatic: The Han king massed forces from five feudal states, totaling 560,000, and launched an eastern campaign against Chu.

sentences.zh: 羽聞之，即令諸將擊齊，而自以精兵三萬人南從魯出胡陵。

translations.literal: When Yu heard this, he immediately ordered generals to attack Qi, while he himself with elite thirty thousand troops went south from Lu and came out at Huling.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Yu heard, he ordered his generals to continue the Qi campaign while he personally led 30,000 elite troops south from Lu through Huling.

sentences.zh: 漢王皆已破彭城，收其貨賂美人，日置酒高會。

translations.literal: The King of Han had already broken Pengcheng and gathered its goods, bribes, and beauties, daily setting wine and grand feasts.
translations.idiomatic: The Han king had already taken Pengcheng and spent his days in lavish drinking, enjoying captured wealth and women.

sentences.zh: 羽乃從蕭晨擊漢軍而東，至彭城，日中，大破漢軍。

translations.literal: Yu then from Xiao attacked the Han army at dawn and eastward; reaching Pengcheng by noon, he greatly defeated the Han army.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu launched a dawn strike from Xiao and by midday at Pengcheng shattered the Han army.

sentences.zh: 漢軍皆走，迫之穀、泗水。

translations.literal: The Han army all fled; [Chu] pressed them to the Gu and Si rivers.
translations.idiomatic: The Han army collapsed in flight, and Chu pursued them to the Gu and Si.

sentences.zh: 漢軍皆南走山，楚又追擊至靈辟東睢水上。

translations.literal: The Han army all fled south to the hills; Chu pursued again to the Sui River east of Lingbi.
translations.idiomatic: Han forces fled south into the hills, but Chu chased them to the Sui River east of Lingbi.

sentences.zh: 漢軍卻，為楚所擠，多殺。

translations.literal: The Han army gave way and was pushed by Chu, with many killed.
translations.idiomatic: The Han lines buckled under Chu pressure, and many were slaughtered.

sentences.zh: 漢卒十餘萬皆入睢水，睢水為不流。

translations.literal: More than one hundred thousand Han soldiers all fell into the Sui River, and the Sui River was blocked from flowing.
translations.idiomatic: Over a hundred thousand Han troops were driven into the Sui, so many that the river itself ran clogged.

sentences.zh: 漢王乃與數十騎遁去。

translations.literal: The Han king then escaped with only several dozen cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: The Han king fled with only a few dozen riders.

sentences.zh: 語在高紀。

translations.literal: The account is in the Basic Annals of Gao.
translations.idiomatic: Details are in the Basic Annals of Gaozu.

sentences.zh: 太公、呂后間求漢王，反遇楚軍。

translations.literal: The Grand Sire and Empress Lü sought the Han king and instead encountered Chu troops.
translations.idiomatic: The Han king's father and Empress Lü went searching for him and fell into Chu hands.

sentences.zh: 楚軍與歸，羽常置軍中。

translations.literal: The Chu army brought them back, and Yu often kept them in camp.
translations.idiomatic: They were taken into Chu custody, and Xiang Yu kept them with the army.

sentences.zh: 漢王稍收散卒，蕭何亦發關中卒悉詣滎陽，戰京、索間，敗楚。

translations.literal: The Han king gradually gathered scattered troops; Xiao He also dispatched all Guanzhong troops to Xingyang. Fighting between Jing and Suo, they defeated Chu.
translations.idiomatic: The Han king slowly regrouped his scattered men, while Xiao He sent reinforcements from Guanzhong to Xingyang; in battles between Jing and Suo they beat Chu.

sentences.zh: 楚以故不能過滎陽而西。

translations.literal: For this reason Chu could not pass Xingyang and go west.
translations.idiomatic: As a result, Chu could not break past Xingyang to the west.

sentences.zh: 漢軍滎陽，築甬道，取敖倉食。

translations.literal: The Han army at Xingyang built supply corridors and took provisions from Ao Granary.
translations.idiomatic: At Xingyang, Han built protected supply lines and drew grain from Aocang.

sentences.zh: 三年，羽數擊絕漢甬道，漢王食乏，請和，割滎陽以西為漢。

translations.literal: In the third year, Yu repeatedly struck and cut Han's supply corridors. The Han king lacked food and requested peace, ceding west of Xingyang to Han.
translations.idiomatic: In the third year Xiang Yu repeatedly severed Han's supply lines. Starving, Liu Bang asked for peace, offering to keep only lands west of Xingyang.

sentences.zh: 羽欲聽之。

translations.literal: Yu wished to accept it.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu was inclined to accept.

sentences.zh: 歷陽侯范增曰：「漢易與耳，今不取，後必悔之。」

translations.literal: Marquis Liyang Fan Zeng said: "Han is easy to deal with; if not taken now, we will surely regret it later."
translations.idiomatic: Fan Zeng, marquis of Liyang, warned: "Han can still be crushed. If we let this pass now, we will regret it."

sentences.zh: 羽乃急圍滎陽。

translations.literal: Yu then urgently encircled Xingyang.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu tightened the siege of Xingyang.

sentences.zh: 漢王患之，乃與陳平金四萬斤以間楚君臣。

translations.literal: The Han king worried over this, and gave Chen Ping forty thousand jin of gold to sow discord among Chu ruler and ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bang, in distress, gave Chen Ping forty thousand jin of gold to drive a wedge through Chu's leadership.

sentences.zh: 語在陳平傳。

translations.literal: The account is in the biography of Chen Ping.
translations.idiomatic: This episode is recorded in Chen Ping's biography.

sentences.zh: 項羽以故疑范增，稍奪之權。

translations.literal: For this reason Xiang Yu suspected Fan Zeng and gradually stripped his authority.
translations.idiomatic: As a result, Xiang Yu began to distrust Fan Zeng and steadily cut his power.

sentences.zh: 范增怒曰：「天下事大定矣，君王自為之！

translations.literal: Fan Zeng angrily said: "The affairs of the realm are largely settled; Your Majesty may handle them yourself.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Zeng said in anger, "The realm is nearly decided. Your Majesty can manage it yourself.

sentences.zh: 願賜骸骨歸。」

translations.literal: I ask to be granted my bones and return."
translations.idiomatic: Grant me leave to retire home."

sentences.zh: 行未至彭城，疽發背死。

translations.literal: Before reaching Pengcheng, a carbuncle erupted on his back and he died.
translations.idiomatic: He died before reaching Pengcheng, struck down by a back carbuncle.

sentences.zh: 於是漢將紀信詐為漢王出降，以誑楚軍，故漢王得與數十騎從西門出。

translations.literal: Then Han general Ji Xin pretended to be the Han king and went out to surrender, deceiving Chu forces, so the Han king could leave by the west gate with several dozen cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: Han general Ji Xin impersonated Liu Bang and surrendered to mislead Chu, allowing the real king to slip out the west gate with a few dozen riders.

sentences.zh: 令周苛、樅公、魏豹守滎陽。

translations.literal: He ordered Zhou Ke, Lord Cong, and Wei Bao to guard Xingyang.
translations.idiomatic: He left Zhou Ke, Lord Cong, and Wei Bao to hold Xingyang.

sentences.zh: 漢王西入關收兵，還出宛、葉間，與九江王黥布行收兵。

translations.literal: The Han king entered the passes westward to gather troops, then came out between Wan and Ye, and together with King Qingbu of Jiujiang gathered troops as they moved.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bang withdrew west into the passes to raise forces, then reemerged between Wan and Ye, rallying troops together with Ying Bu, king of Jiujiang.

sentences.zh: 羽聞之，即引兵南。

translations.literal: When Yu heard this, he immediately led troops south.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Yu heard, he immediately marched south.

sentences.zh: 漢王堅壁不與戰。

translations.literal: The King of Han entrenched firmly and would not engage in battle.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han fortified his position and refused battle.

sentences.zh: 是時，彭越渡睢，與項聲、薛公戰下邳，殺薛公。

translations.literal: At this time Peng Yue crossed the Sui and fought Xiang Sheng and Lord Xue at Xiapi, killing Lord Xue.
translations.idiomatic: At this time Peng Yue crossed the Sui, fought Xiang Sheng and Lord Xue at Xiapi, and killed Lord Xue.

sentences.zh: 羽乃東擊彭越。

translations.literal: Yu then went east to strike Peng Yue.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu turned east to attack Peng Yue.

sentences.zh: 漢王亦引兵北軍成皋。

translations.literal: The King of Han also led troops north to encamp at Chenggao.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han also marched north and made camp at Chenggao.

sentences.zh: 羽已破走彭越，引兵西下滎陽城，亨周苛，殺樅公，虜韓王信，進圍成皋。

translations.literal: After Yu had defeated and driven off Peng Yue, he led troops west to Xingyang, boiled Zhou Ke alive, killed Lord Cong, captured Han king Xin, and advanced to besiege Chenggao.
translations.idiomatic: After routing Peng Yue, Xiang Yu moved west on Xingyang, executed Zhou Ke by boiling, killed Lord Cong, captured Han Xin of Han, and then besieged Chenggao.

sentences.zh: 漢王跳，獨與滕公得出。

translations.literal: The Han king fled by jumping out, and only with Lord Teng escaped.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bang escaped by a desperate breakout, getting out only with Lord Teng.

sentences.zh: 北渡河，至修武，從張耳、韓信。

translations.literal: He crossed the River north to Xiuwu and joined Zhang Er and Han Xin.
translations.idiomatic: He crossed north of the river to Xiuwu and joined Zhang Er and Han Xin.

sentences.zh: 楚遂拔成皋。

translations.literal: Chu then took Chenggao.
translations.idiomatic: Chu then captured Chenggao.

sentences.zh: 漢王得韓信軍，留止，使盧綰、劉賈渡白馬津入楚地，佐彭越共擊破楚軍燕郭西，燒其積聚，攻下梁地十餘城。

translations.literal: The Han king obtained Han Xin's army and remained there, sending Lu Wan and Liu Jia across Baima Ford into Chu territory to aid Peng Yue in jointly defeating Chu west of Yankuo, burning their stores and taking over ten cities in Liang lands.
translations.idiomatic: After gaining Han Xin's army, Liu Bang held position and sent Lu Wan and Liu Jia across Baima Ford into Chu territory; together with Peng Yue they beat Chu west of Yankuo, burned supplies, and took more than ten Liang cities.

sentences.zh: 羽聞之，謂海春侯大司馬曹咎曰：「

translations.literal: When Yu heard this, he said to Marquis Haichun, Grand Marshal Cao Jiu: "
translations.idiomatic: Hearing this, Xiang Yu told Grand Marshal Cao Jiu, Marquis of Haichun: "

sentences.zh: 謹守成皋。

translations.literal: Guard Chenggao carefully.
translations.idiomatic: Hold Chenggao carefully.

sentences.zh: 即漢欲挑戰，慎毋與戰，勿令得東而已。

translations.literal: If Han tries to challenge battle, be sure not to fight; just do not let them get east."
translations.idiomatic: If Han provokes you, do not engage. Just keep them from moving east."

sentences.zh: 我十五日必定梁地，復從將軍。」

translations.literal: In fifteen days I will certainly settle the land of Liang and return to join the generals."
translations.idiomatic: In fifteen days I will certainly settle the land of Liang and return to join the generals.''

sentences.zh: 於是引兵東。

translations.literal: Thereupon he led troops east.
translations.idiomatic: With that, he took troops eastward.

sentences.zh: 四年，羽擊陳留、外黃，外黃不下。

translations.literal: In the fourth year, Yu attacked Chenliu and Waihuang; Waihuang did not fall.
translations.idiomatic: In the fourth year, Xiang Yu attacked Chenliu and Waihuang, but Waihuang held out.

sentences.zh: 數日降，羽悉令男子年十五以上詣城東，欲阬之。

translations.literal: After some days it surrendered; Yu ordered all males fifteen and above to go east of the city, intending to bury them alive.
translations.idiomatic: After several days the city surrendered, and Xiang Yu ordered every male fifteen or older assembled east of the walls for mass execution by burial.

sentences.zh: 外黃令舍人兒年十三，往說羽曰：「彭越強劫外黃，外黃恐，故且降，待大王。

translations.literal: A retainer boy of Waihuang magistrate, age thirteen, went to persuade Yu: "Peng Yue forced Waihuang by violence; Waihuang was afraid, and so it only surrendered, waiting for Your Majesty.
translations.idiomatic: A thirteen-year-old servant of the Waihuang magistrate came to remonstrate: "Peng Yue coerced Waihuang by force; out of fear we submitted only until Your Majesty arrived.

sentences.zh: 大王至，又皆阬之，百姓豈有所歸心哉！

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty has come and still buries them all alive; how could the people then have hearts to return?"
translations.idiomatic: If you now bury them anyway, how can any people still turn their hearts to you?"

sentences.zh: 從此以東，梁地十餘城皆恐，莫肯下矣。」

translations.literal: From this eastward, Liang territory more than ten cities all fear, none willing to surrender.'
translations.idiomatic: East of here, all the cities in Liang territory—more than ten of them—will be terrified and none will surrender.'

sentences.zh: 羽然其言，乃赦外黃當阬者。

translations.literal: Yu approved his words, and so pardoned those of Waihuang who were to be buried.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu accepted his argument and spared those marked for execution.

sentences.zh: 而東至睢陽，聞之皆爭下。

translations.literal: Then when he went east to Suiyang, hearing this, all strove to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: After that, as he advanced east to Suiyang, cities hearing of his clemency rushed to surrender.

sentences.zh: 漢果數挑楚軍戰，楚軍不出。

translations.literal: Han indeed repeatedly provoked Chu army to battle, Chu army did not come out.
translations.idiomatic: As expected, Han repeatedly challenged the Chu army to battle, but the Chu army refused to come out.

sentences.zh: 使人辱之，五六日，大司馬怒，渡兵氾水。

translations.literal: [Han] sent men to insult him for five or six days; the Grand Marshal grew enraged and crossed troops over the Si River.
translations.idiomatic: Han taunted him for five or six days; Cao Jiu, enraged, led troops across the Si.

sentences.zh: 卒半渡，漢擊，大破之，盡得楚國金玉貨賂。

translations.literal: When half the troops had crossed, Han attacked and greatly defeated them, obtaining all Chu gold, jade, and valuables.
translations.idiomatic: With only half the army across, Han struck and crushed them, seizing Chu's gold, jade, and treasures.

sentences.zh: 大司馬咎、長史欣皆自剄氾水上。

translations.literal: Grand Marshal Jiu and Chief Clerk Xin both cut their throats on the Si River.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Marshal Cao Jiu and Chief Clerk Sima Xin both committed suicide at the Si.

sentences.zh: 咎故蘄獄掾，欣故塞王，羽信任之。

translations.literal: Jiu had formerly been prison clerk of Qi, Xin formerly king of Sai; Yu trusted and employed them.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Jiu had once been a prison clerk in Qi, and Xin had been king of Sai; Xiang Yu had trusted both deeply.

sentences.zh: 羽至睢陽，聞咎等破，則引兵還，漢軍方圍鍾離昧於滎陽東，羽軍至，漢軍畏楚，盡走險阻。

translations.literal: When Yu reached Suiyang and heard Jiu and others defeated, he led troops back. Han armies were then besieging Zhongli Mo east of Xingyang; when Yu's army arrived, Han troops feared Chu and all fled into difficult terrain.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Yu reached Suiyang and learned of their defeat, he turned back. Han was then besieging Zhongli Mo east of Xingyang, but when Xiang Yu arrived Han forces fled into rough ground.

sentences.zh: 羽亦軍廣武相守，乃為高俎，置太公其上，告漢王曰：「今不急下，吾亨太公。」

translations.literal: Yu also camped at Guangwu facing [Han], then made a high chopping block, placed the Grand Sire on it, and told the Han king: "If you do not surrender quickly, I will boil the Grand Sire."
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu then faced Han at Guangwu, set up a high execution block, placed Liu Bang's father on it, and threatened: "Surrender now, or I boil him alive."

sentences.zh: 漢王曰：「吾與若俱北面受命懷王，約為兄弟，吾翁即汝翁。

translations.literal: The Han king said: "I and you together faced north and received mandate from King Huai, agreeing as brothers; my old man is your old man.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bang replied, "You and I both received command from King Huai and swore brotherhood. My father is your father too.

sentences.zh: 必欲亨乃翁，幸分我一盃羹。」

translations.literal: If you must boil your old man, please share me a bowl of soup."
translations.idiomatic: If you insist on boiling him, send me a bowl of the broth."

sentences.zh: 羽怒，欲殺之。

translations.literal: Yu was enraged and wanted to kill him.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu flew into a rage and wanted to kill him.

sentences.zh: 項伯曰：「天下事未可知。

translations.literal: Xiang Bo said: "The affairs of the realm are not yet knowable.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Bo said, "The outcome of the realm is still uncertain.

sentences.zh: 且為天下者不顧家，雖殺之無益，但益怨耳。」

translations.literal: Moreover, one who contends for the realm does not heed family; even if you kill him it gives no benefit, only increases resentment."
translations.idiomatic: And those fighting for the world do not yield over family alone. Killing him gains nothing and only deepens hatred."

sentences.zh: 羽從之。

translations.literal: Yu followed this.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu accepted that advice.

sentences.zh: 乃使人謂漢王曰：「天下匈匈，徒以吾兩人願與王挑戰，決雌雄，毋徒罷天下父子為也。」

translations.literal: Then he sent someone to tell the Han king: "All-under-Heaven is in turmoil only because of us two; I wish to challenge the king to decide male and female [victory], and not vainly exhaust the fathers and sons of the world."
translations.idiomatic: He then sent word: "The realm suffers only because we two remain unresolved. Let us fight one-on-one and settle it, instead of exhausting the fathers and sons of the world."

sentences.zh: 漢王笑謝曰：「吾寧鬥智，不能鬥力。」

translations.literal: The Han king smiled and declined, saying: "I would rather fight with strategy and cannot fight with strength."
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bang laughed and refused: "I compete with strategy, not brute strength."

sentences.zh: 羽令壯士出挑戰。

translations.literal: Yu ordered a strong warrior to come out and challenge.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu sent out a champion for single combat.

sentences.zh: 漢有善騎射曰樓煩，楚挑戰，三合，樓煩輒射殺之。

translations.literal: Han had one skilled in mounted archery named Loufan. When Chu challenged, after three rounds Loufan would shoot and kill them.
translations.idiomatic: Han had a mounted archer called Loufan; whenever Chu sent challengers, he shot them dead within three bouts.

sentences.zh: 羽大怒，自被甲持戟挑戰。

translations.literal: Yu was greatly enraged, put on armor himself, and held halberd to challenge.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu, enraged, donned armor and personally came out with a halberd.

sentences.zh: 樓煩欲射，羽瞋目叱之。

translations.literal: Loufan wished to shoot; Yu glared and shouted at him.
translations.idiomatic: Loufan drew to shoot, but Xiang Yu glared and roared at him.

sentences.zh: 樓煩目不能視，手不能發，走還入壁，不敢復出。

translations.literal: Loufan's eyes could not see, his hand could not release [arrow]; he ran back into the wall and did not dare come out again.
translations.idiomatic: Loufan's gaze faltered, his hand froze, and he fled back behind the wall, never daring to emerge again.

sentences.zh: 漢王使間問之，乃羽也。

translations.literal: The Han king sent spies to ask, and it was Yu indeed.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bang sent men to confirm it, and it was indeed Xiang Yu.

sentences.zh: 漢王大驚。

translations.literal: Han king greatly astonished.
translations.idiomatic: The Han king was greatly shocked.

sentences.zh: 於是羽與漢王相與臨廣武間而語。

translations.literal: Thereupon Yu and the Han king approached each other between Guangwu and spoke.
translations.idiomatic: Then Xiang Yu and Liu Bang came face-to-face between the Guangwu lines and spoke.

sentences.zh: 漢王數羽十罪。

translations.literal: The Han king enumerated ten crimes of Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bang listed Xiang Yu's ten crimes.

sentences.zh: 語在高紀。

translations.literal: The account is in the Basic Annals of Gao.
translations.idiomatic: The full speech appears in the Basic Annals of Gaozu.

sentences.zh: 羽怒，伏弩射傷漢王。

translations.literal: Yu was enraged and shot with a hidden crossbow, wounding the Han king.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu, enraged, loosed a concealed crossbow bolt and wounded Liu Bang.

sentences.zh: 漢王入成皋。

translations.literal: The Han king entered Chenggao.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bang withdrew into Chenggao.

sentences.zh: 時彭越數反梁地，絕楚糧食，又韓信破齊，且欲擊楚。

translations.literal: At this time Peng Yue repeatedly rebelled in Liang lands and cut Chu grain supply; also Han Xin had defeated Qi and was about to strike Chu.
translations.idiomatic: At this time Peng Yue kept rising in Liang and cutting Chu's food routes, while Han Xin had already broken Qi and was preparing to attack Chu.

sentences.zh: 羽使從兄子項它為大將，龍且為裨將，救齊。

translations.literal: Yu sent his elder-cousin's son Xiang Tuo as great general and Long Ju as deputy general to rescue Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu sent his cousin's son Xiang Tuo as commander, with Long Ju as deputy, to relieve Qi.

sentences.zh: 韓信破殺龍且，追至成陽，虜齊王廣。

translations.literal: Han Xin defeated and killed Long Ju, pursued to Chengyang, and captured Qi king Guang.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin defeated and killed Long Ju, pursued to Chengyang, and captured King Guang of Qi.

sentences.zh: 信遂自立為齊王。

translations.literal: Xin then set himself up as king of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin then declared himself king of Qi.

sentences.zh: 羽聞之，恐，使武涉往說信。

translations.literal: When Yu heard this, he feared and sent Wu She to persuade Xin.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Yu heard, he grew alarmed and sent Wu She to persuade Han Xin.

sentences.zh: 語在信傳。

translations.literal: The account is in Xin's biography.
translations.idiomatic: This is recorded in Han Xin's biography.

sentences.zh: 時，漢關中兵益出，食多，羽兵食少。

translations.literal: At this time Han troops from Guanzhong kept increasing and food was abundant, while Yu's troops had little food.
translations.idiomatic: By then Han reinforcements from Guanzhong kept arriving and supplies were plentiful, while Xiang Yu's army was short of grain.

sentences.zh: 漢王使侯公說羽，羽乃與漢王約，中分天下，割鴻溝而西者為漢，東者為楚，歸漢王父母妻子。

translations.literal: The Han king sent Marquis Hou to persuade Yu; Yu then made covenant with the Han king, dividing the realm in the middle: west of Hong Canal to Han, east to Chu, and returned the Han king's parents, wife, and children.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bang sent Marquis Hou to negotiate, and Xiang Yu agreed to divide the empire at the Hong Canal, west to Han and east to Chu, and to return Liu Bang's family.

sentences.zh: 已約，羽解而東。

translations.literal: After agreement, Yu released and went east.
translations.idiomatic: After concluding terms, Xiang Yu withdrew east.

sentences.zh: 五年，漢王進兵追羽，至故陵，復為羽所敗。

translations.literal: In the fifth year, the Han king advanced troops pursuing Yu to Guling, and was again defeated by Yu.
translations.idiomatic: In the fifth year Liu Bang advanced in pursuit as far as Guling, but Xiang Yu defeated him again.

sentences.zh: 漢王用張良計，致齊王信、建成侯、彭越兵，乃劉賈入楚地，圍壽春。

translations.literal: The Han king used Zhang Liang's plan, bringing troops of King Xin of Qi, Marquis Jiancheng, and Peng Yue; then Liu Jia entered Chu territory and besieged Shouchun.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bang then adopted Zhang Liang's strategy, summoning forces from King Xin of Qi, the Marquis of Jiancheng, and Peng Yue, while Liu Jia pushed into Chu and besieged Shouchun.

sentences.zh: 大司馬周殷叛楚，舉九江兵隨劉賈，迎黥布，與齊梁諸侯皆大會。

translations.literal: Grand Marshal Zhou Yin betrayed Chu, raising Jiujiang troops to follow Liu Jia, welcoming Qingbu, and all the lords of Qi and Liang gathered in great assembly.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Marshal Zhou Yin defected from Chu with Jiujiang troops to join Liu Jia, brought Ying Bu over, and the lords of Qi and Liang assembled in force.

sentences.zh: 羽壁垓下，軍少食盡。

translations.literal: Yu camped at Gaixia; the army was few and provisions exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu encamped at Gaixia with dwindling troops and exhausted supplies.

sentences.zh: 漢帥諸侯兵圍之數重。

translations.literal: Han led the lords' armies to encircle him in multiple layers.
translations.idiomatic: Han and the feudal armies surrounded him in several rings.

sentences.zh: 羽夜聞漢軍四面皆楚歌，乃驚曰：「漢皆已得楚乎？

translations.literal: At night Yu heard Han armies singing Chu songs on all four sides, and was startled, saying: "Has Han already gained all Chu?
translations.idiomatic: At night Xiang Yu heard Chu songs from every side of Han's encirclement and cried out, "Has Han already taken all Chu?

sentences.zh: 是何楚人多也！」

translations.literal: How are there so many Chu men!"
translations.idiomatic: How can there be so many men of Chu?"

sentences.zh: 起飲帳中。

translations.literal: He rose and drank in his tent.
translations.idiomatic: He rose and drank inside his tent.

sentences.zh: 有美人姓虞氏，常幸從；

translations.literal: There was a beauty surnamed Yu, often favored at his side.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Yu, his favored consort, was with him as always.

sentences.zh: 駿馬名騅，常騎。

translations.literal: His fine horse was named Zhui, which he always rode.
translations.idiomatic: His famed steed Zhui, which he always rode, was there too.

sentences.zh: 乃悲歌忼慨，自為歌詩曰：「力拔山兮氣蓋世，時不利兮騅不逝。

translations.literal: Then in grief and passion he sang sadly, composing a song: "My strength uproots mountains, my spirit overmasters the age; but times are against me, and Zhui will not run.
translations.idiomatic: In grief he sang impromptu: "My strength could pull mountains, my spirit overshadowed the age; yet fortune is against me, and Zhui no longer runs.

sentences.zh: 騅不逝兮可柰何！

translations.literal: If Zhui will not run, what can be done!
translations.idiomatic: If Zhui no longer runs, what can I do?

sentences.zh: 虞兮虞兮柰若何！」

translations.literal: Yu, O Yu, what can be done with you!"
translations.idiomatic: Yu, my Yu, what am I to do with you?"

sentences.zh: 歌數曲，美人和之。

translations.literal: He sang several times; the beauty harmonized with him.
translations.idiomatic: He sang it again and again, and Lady Yu sang in harmony.

sentences.zh: 羽泣下數行，左右皆泣，莫能仰視。

translations.literal: Yu wept with tears falling in several streams; all around him wept, and none could raise their eyes.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu's tears streamed down; all those beside him wept and none dared look up.

sentences.zh: 於是羽遂上馬，戲下騎從者八百餘人，夜直潰圍南出馳。

translations.literal: Thereupon Yu mounted his horse; the riders following under his standard were over eight hundred. At night he broke the encirclement straight through south and rode off.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu mounted up, and with more than eight hundred riders under his banner broke south through the encirclement in the night.

sentences.zh: 平明，漢軍乃覺之，令騎將灌嬰以五千騎追羽。

translations.literal: At dawn the Han army then noticed it and ordered cavalry commander Guan Ying with five thousand riders to pursue Yu.
translations.idiomatic: At daybreak Han realized he had escaped and sent cavalry commander Guan Ying with five thousand horse in pursuit.

sentences.zh: 羽渡淮，騎能屬者百餘人。

translations.literal: Yu crossed the Huai; riders still attached to him were only over one hundred.
translations.idiomatic: After crossing the Huai, Xiang Yu had barely more than one hundred riders left with him.

sentences.zh: 羽至陰陵，迷失道，問一田父，田父紿曰「左」。

translations.literal: When Yu reached Yinling, he lost his way and asked an old farmer, who tricked him by saying "left."
translations.idiomatic: At Yinling, Xiang Yu lost the road. He asked a farmer for directions, and the man deliberately sent him left.

sentences.zh: 左，乃陷大澤中，以故漢追及之。

translations.literal: Going left, he fell into great marshes, and for this reason Han pursuit caught up.
translations.idiomatic: That turn led him into marshland, allowing the Han pursuers to catch up.

sentences.zh: 羽復引而東，至東城，乃有二十八騎。

translations.literal: Yu then led east again; on reaching Dongcheng, he had only twenty-eight riders.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu turned east again, and by Dongcheng only twenty-eight horsemen remained.

sentences.zh: 追者數千，羽自度不得脫，謂其騎曰：「吾起兵至今八歲矣，身七十餘戰，所當者破，所擊者服，未嘗敗北，遂伯有天下。

translations.literal: With thousands pursuing, Yu judged he could not escape and said to his riders: "Since I raised troops to now, eight years have passed. I have fought over seventy battles: those I faced were broken, those I struck submitted. I have never once been defeated, and so I became hegemon over the realm.
translations.idiomatic: With thousands in pursuit, Xiang Yu knew he could not get away. He told his riders: "Eight years since we rose, over seventy battles fought, and never a defeat. All who faced me were broken, all I attacked submitted. That is how I held the realm.

sentences.zh: 然今卒困於此，此天亡我，非戰之罪也。

translations.literal: Yet now at last I am trapped here. This is Heaven destroying me, not the fault of battle.
translations.idiomatic: Yet now I am cornered here. Heaven destroys me; this is not a failure of command.

sentences.zh: 今日固決死，願為諸軍快戰，必三勝，斬將，艾旗，乃後死，使諸君知我非用兵罪，天亡我也。」

translations.literal: Today I am fixed on dying. I ask to fight sharply for you: we will surely win three times, cut down commanders, and topple banners before I die, so you may know my military use is not at fault; Heaven destroys me."
translations.idiomatic: Today I am resolved to die. I will win three more charges, kill their officers, and cut their standards before I fall, so you all know this is Heaven's decree, not my military fault."

sentences.zh: 於是引其騎因四隤山而為圜陳外嚮。

translations.literal: He then led his riders, using Sihui Mountain, to form a circular array facing outward.
translations.idiomatic: He drew his riders around Sihui Mountain in a ring formation facing out on all sides.

sentences.zh: 漢騎圍之數重。

translations.literal: Han cavalry encircled them in several layers.
translations.idiomatic: Han cavalry surrounded them in multiple rings.

sentences.zh: 羽謂其騎曰：「吾為公取彼一將。」

translations.literal: Yu told his riders: "I will take one of their commanders for you."
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu said, "Watch me take one of their commanders."

sentences.zh: 令四面騎馳下，期山東為三處。

translations.literal: Commanded four sides horsemen gallop down, agreed east of mountain as three places.
translations.idiomatic: He commanded the horsemen on all four sides to charge down and agreed to rally at three places east of the mountain.

sentences.zh: 於是羽大呼馳下，漢軍皆披靡。

translations.literal: Then Yu shouted loudly and galloped down; Han forces all fell back in rout.
translations.idiomatic: With a great roar, Xiang Yu charged downhill, and Han ranks broke before him.

sentences.zh: 遂殺漢一將。

translations.literal: He then killed a Han commander.
translations.idiomatic: He cut down a Han commander.

sentences.zh: 是時，楊喜為郎騎，追羽，羽還叱之，喜人馬俱驚，辟易數里。

translations.literal: At that time Yang Xi, serving in mounted attendant corps, pursued Yu. Yu turned and shouted at him; Yang Xi's man and horse were both terrified and withdrew several li.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xi, a Han mounted attendant, gave chase. Xiang Yu turned and bellowed; Yang Xi and his horse panicked and fell back several miles.

sentences.zh: 與其騎會三處。

translations.literal: He met his riders at the three points.
translations.idiomatic: He rejoined his riders at the three agreed points.

sentences.zh: 漢軍不知羽所居，分軍為三，復圍之。

translations.literal: The Han army did not know where Yu was, split into three forces, and encircled again.
translations.idiomatic: Han forces lost track of Xiang Yu, split into three columns, and closed in again.

sentences.zh: 羽乃馳，復斬漢一都尉，殺數十百人。

translations.literal: Yu then galloped and again beheaded one Han commandant, killing several dozen to over a hundred men.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu charged again, killed another Han officer, and cut down dozens upon dozens of men.

sentences.zh: 復聚其騎，亡兩騎。

translations.literal: He regrouped his riders again, having lost two riders.
translations.idiomatic: He regrouped once more, now missing only two riders.

sentences.zh: 乃謂騎曰：「何如？」

translations.literal: He then said to his riders: "How is it?"
translations.idiomatic: He asked, "Well?"

sentences.zh: 騎皆服曰：「如大王言。」

translations.literal: All riders submitted, saying: "Just as Your Majesty said."
translations.idiomatic: The riders all answered in awe, "Exactly as you said, Great King."

sentences.zh: 於是羽遂引東，欲渡烏江。

translations.literal: Then Yu led east, wishing to cross the Wu River.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu then moved east, intending to cross the Wu River.

sentences.zh: 烏江亭長檥船待，謂羽曰：「江東雖小，地方千里，眾數十萬，亦足王也。

translations.literal: The Wu River pavilion chief moored a boat and waited, telling Yu: "Though Jiangdong is small, its land is a thousand li and its people are in the hundreds of thousands; it is enough to be king there.
translations.idiomatic: The Wu River ferrymaster had a boat ready and urged him: "Jiangdong may be small, but it has broad lands and hundreds of thousands of people, enough for kingship.

sentences.zh: 願大王急渡。

translations.literal: Wish Your Majesty urgently cross.
translations.idiomatic: I urge Your Majesty to cross quickly.'

sentences.zh: 今獨臣有船，漢軍至，亡以渡。」

translations.literal: Now only I have a boat; when Han troops arrive, there will be no way to cross."
translations.idiomatic: Right now only this one boat is available. Once Han arrives, you will not be able to cross."

sentences.zh: 羽笑曰：「乃天亡我，何渡為！

translations.literal: Yu laughed and said: "Since Heaven destroys me, why cross?
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu laughed: "If Heaven means to destroy me, what use is crossing?

sentences.zh: 且籍與江東子弟八千人渡而西，今亡一人還，縱江東父兄憐而王我，我何面目見之哉？

translations.literal: Moreover, I, Ji, led eight thousand Jiangdong youths across westward, and now not one man returns. Even if Jiangdong fathers and elders pity me and make me king, with what face could I see them?
translations.idiomatic: I led eight thousand sons of Jiangdong west, and not one returns. Even if their fathers and elders took pity and made me king again, how could I face them?

sentences.zh: 縱彼不言，籍獨不愧於心乎！」

translations.literal: Even if they do not speak, Ji alone not ashamed in heart?'
translations.idiomatic: Even if they don't say anything, wouldn't I feel ashamed in my own heart?''

sentences.zh: 謂亭長曰：「吾知公長者也，吾騎此馬五歲，所當亡敵，嘗一日千里，吾不忍殺，以賜公。」

translations.literal: He told the pavilion chief: "I know you are an elder worthy. I have ridden this horse five years; where it charged, no enemy stood. It once ran a thousand li in one day. I cannot bear to kill it, so I give it to you."
translations.idiomatic: He told the ferrymaster: "I know you are a good man. I have ridden this horse for five years; no enemy withstood it, and it has covered a thousand li in a day. I cannot bear to kill it, so I give it to you."

sentences.zh: 乃令騎皆去馬，步持短兵接戰。

translations.literal: He then ordered all riders to dismount and fight on foot with short weapons.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered his men to abandon their horses and fight on foot with short arms.

sentences.zh: 羽獨所殺漢軍數百人。

translations.literal: Yu alone killed several hundred Han soldiers.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu himself killed several hundred Han men.

sentences.zh: 羽亦被十餘創。

translations.literal: Yu also received more than ten wounds.
translations.idiomatic: He too took more than ten wounds.

sentences.zh: 顧見漢騎司馬呂馬童曰：「若非吾故人乎？」

translations.literal: Looked back saw Han cavalry commander Lu Matong, said: 'You not my old friend?'
translations.idiomatic: Looking back, he saw the Han cavalry commander Lu Matong and said: 'Aren't you my old friend?'

sentences.zh: 馬童面之，指王翳曰：「此項王也。」

translations.literal: Matong faced him, pointed to Wang Yi said: 'This is King Xiang.'
translations.idiomatic: Lu Matong faced him and pointed to Wang Yi, saying: 'This is King Xiang.'

sentences.zh: 羽乃曰：「吾聞漢購我頭千金，邑萬戶，吾為公得。」

translations.literal: Yu then said: "I hear Han offers a thousand jin and a city of ten thousand households for my head; I will get it for you."
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu said, "Han offers a thousand jin and a ten-thousand-household fief for my head. I will give you that reward."

sentences.zh: 乃自剄。

translations.literal: He then cut his own throat.
translations.idiomatic: Then he cut his own throat.

sentences.zh: 王翳取其頭，亂相輮蹈爭羽相殺者數十人。

translations.literal: Wang Yi took his head. In the chaos they trampled each other, and dozens died fighting over Yu's remains.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Yi seized Xiang Yu's head, and in the scramble men crushed and killed one another by the dozens to claim the body.

sentences.zh: 最後楊喜、呂馬童、郎中呂勝、楊武各得其一體。

translations.literal: In the end Yang Xi, Lü Matong, Gentleman-in-attendance Lü Sheng, and Yang Wu each obtained one part of his body.
translations.idiomatic: In the end Yang Xi, Lü Matong, Gentleman Lü Sheng, and Yang Wu each took a part of his corpse.

sentences.zh: 故分其地以封五人，皆為列侯。

translations.literal: Therefore his lands were divided to enfeoff five men, all becoming ranked marquises.
translations.idiomatic: So his former lands were split among five claimants, all enfeoffed as marquises.

sentences.zh: 漢王乃以魯公號葬羽於穀城。

translations.literal: The Han king then buried Yu at Gucheng with the title Duke of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: The Han king had Xiang Yu buried at Gucheng with honors under the title Duke of Lu.

sentences.zh: 諸項支屬皆不誅。

translations.literal: All collateral members of the Xiang clan were not executed.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiang clan's collateral branches were spared.

sentences.zh: 封項伯等四人為列侯，賜姓劉氏。

translations.literal: He enfeoffed Xiang Bo and three others as marquises and granted them the surname Liu.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Bo and three others were made marquises and granted the imperial surname Liu.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：昔賈生之過秦曰：

translations.literal: Appraisal says: Formerly Master Jia in his "On Qin's Errors" said:
translations.idiomatic: Appraisal: Jia Yi once wrote in "Faulting Qin":

sentences.zh: 秦孝公據殽函之固，擁雍州之地，君臣固守而闚周室，有席卷天下，包舉宇內，囊括四海，并吞八荒之心。

translations.literal: Duke Xiao of Qin occupied the stronghold of Xiao and Han and held the land of Yongzhou. Ruler and ministers firmly guarded while eyeing the Zhou house, harboring intent to sweep all under Heaven, encompass the realm, bag the four seas, and swallow the eight directions.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Xiao of Qin held the natural defenses of Xiao and Han and controlled Yongzhou. Ruler and ministers guarded it tightly while watching the Zhou court, bent on sweeping up the world and absorbing all within the four seas.

sentences.zh: 當是時也，商君佐之，內立法度，務耕織，修守戰之備，外連衡而鬥諸侯。

translations.literal: At that time Lord Shang assisted him: inside, he established laws and institutions, emphasized farming and weaving, and repaired preparations for defense and war; outside, he allied horizontally and fought the lords.
translations.idiomatic: Shang Yang aided him by imposing internal laws, prioritizing agriculture and textiles, and strengthening military readiness, while abroad using alliance strategy to pit the feudal states against one another.

sentences.zh: 於是秦人拱手而取西河之外。

translations.literal: Thus Qin men took lands west of the Yellow River with folded hands.
translations.idiomatic: Thus Qin seized the lands west of the Yellow River with apparent ease.

sentences.zh: 孝公既沒，惠文、武、昭襄蒙故業，因遺策，南取漢中，西舉巴蜀，東割膏腴之地，收要害之郡。

translations.literal: After Duke Xiao died, Kings Huiwen, Wu, and Zhaoxiang inherited the old enterprise and followed the remaining strategy: taking Hanzhong in the south, seizing Ba and Shu in the west, cutting fertile lands in the east, and taking commanderies at strategic choke points.
translations.idiomatic: After Duke Xiao, Kings Huiwen, Wu, and Zhaoxiang built on inherited gains and strategy, taking Hanzhong, then Ba and Shu, then fertile eastern lands and key strategic commanderies.

sentences.zh: 諸侯恐懼，會盟而謀弱秦，不愛珍器重寶肥饒之地，以致天下之士。

translations.literal: The lords were terrified, formed alliances and plotted to weaken Qin, not sparing precious objects, heavy treasures, or rich lands in order to attract talent from the realm.
translations.idiomatic: The feudal states, alarmed, swore alliances to weaken Qin and spent treasure, land, and privilege to recruit the best men under heaven.

sentences.zh: 合從締交，相與為一。

translations.literal: They united in vertical alliance and bound ties, acting as one.
translations.idiomatic: They forged vertical alliances and acted as a single bloc.

sentences.zh: 當此之時，齊有孟嘗，趙有平原，楚有春申，魏有信陵。

translations.literal: At that time Qi had Lord Mengchang, Zhao had Lord Pingyuan, Chu had Lord Chunshen, and Wei had Lord Xinling.
translations.idiomatic: At that time Qi had Mengchang, Zhao had Pingyuan, Chu had Chunshen, and Wei had Xinling.

sentences.zh: 此四賢者，皆明智而忠信，寬厚而愛人，尊賢重士，約從離橫，兼韓、魏、燕、趙、宋、衛、中山之眾。

translations.literal: These four worthies were all intelligent and faithful, broad and humane, honoring worthies and valuing scholars, binding vertical alliances and breaking horizontal ones, combining the forces of Han, Wei, Yan, Zhao, Song, Wey, and Zhongshan.
translations.idiomatic: These four were wise and trustworthy, generous and people-minded. They honored talent and rallied men, knitting vertical alliances and breaking Qin's horizontal strategy, drawing in Han, Wei, Yan, Zhao, Song, Wey, and Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 於是六國之士有甯越、徐尚、蘇秦、杜赫之屬為之謀，齊明、周最、陳軫、召滑、樓緩、翟景、蘇厲、樂毅之徒通其意，吳起、孫臏、帶他、兒良、王廖、田忌、廉頗、趙奢之朋制其兵。

translations.literal: Then among the six states there were men such as Ning Yue, Xu Shang, Su Qin, and Du He who planned for them; Qi Ming, Zhou Zui, Chen Zhen, Shao Hua, Lou Huan, Zhai Jing, Su Li, and Yue Yi who communicated their intent; and Wu Qi, Sun Bin, Dai Tuo, Er Liang, Wang Liao, Tian Ji, Lian Po, and Zhao She who directed their armies.
translations.idiomatic: So the six states had planners like Ning Yue and Su Qin, diplomats like Chen Zhen and Yue Yi, and commanders like Wu Qi, Sun Bin, Tian Ji, Lian Po, and Zhao She coordinating strategy, policy, and armies.

sentences.zh: 常以十倍之地，百萬之軍，仰關而攻秦。

translations.literal: They regularly, with territory ten times [Qin] and a million troops, looked up at the passes and attacked Qin.
translations.idiomatic: With tenfold land and armies in the millions, they repeatedly attacked Qin through the passes.

sentences.zh: 秦人開關延敵，九國之師遁巡而不敢進。

translations.literal: Qin opened the passes and let in the enemy; the armies of the nine states retreated and dared not advance.
translations.idiomatic: Yet when Qin opened the passes to meet them, the coalition armies of the nine states wavered and would not press forward.

sentences.zh: 秦無亡矢遺鏃之費，而天下已困矣。

translations.literal: Qin bore no cost of lost arrows or spent bolts, yet the realm was already exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: Qin paid almost nothing in men or matériel, while its enemies were already drained.

sentences.zh: 於是從散約敗，爭割地而賂秦。

translations.literal: Then the vertical alliance scattered and covenants failed; they competed to cede land and bribe Qin.
translations.idiomatic: The coalition broke apart, and the states scrambled to cede territory and buy Qin off.

sentences.zh: 秦有餘力而制其弊，追亡逐北，伏尸百萬，流血漂鹵，因利乘便，宰割天下，分裂山河；

translations.literal: Qin had surplus strength to control their weakness, pursuing fugitives and routing defeated forces, with a million corpses and blood flowing to float shields; seizing advantage and opportunity, it carved up the realm and split mountains and rivers.
translations.idiomatic: Qin then exploited their weakness, chasing routed armies and piling up corpses in the hundreds of thousands, blood running like floodwater, and proceeded to carve up the realm.

sentences.zh: 強國請服，弱國入朝。

translations.literal: Strong states asked to submit; weak states entered court.
translations.idiomatic: Strong states requested submission; weak ones came to court.

sentences.zh: 施及孝文、莊襄王，享國之日淺，國家亡事。

translations.literal: This extended to Kings Xiaowen and Zhuangxiang, whose days ruling were short and the state had no affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Through Kings Xiaowen and Zhuangxiang, whose reigns were brief, the state met no major trouble.

sentences.zh: 及至始皇，奮六世之餘烈，振長策而馭宇內，吞二周而亡諸侯，履至尊而制六合，執敲扑以鞭笞天下，威震四海。

translations.literal: When it came to the First Emperor, he roused the remaining force of six generations, brandished the long whip to drive the realm, swallowed the two Zhous and extinguished the lords, trod the supreme seat and controlled the six directions, wielded rod and lash to whip all under Heaven, and his might shook the four seas.
translations.idiomatic: Then the First Emperor inherited six generations of momentum, drove the empire with iron force, extinguished the feudal states, took supreme rule, and ruled all directions by punishment and terror.

sentences.zh: 南取百粵之地，以為桂林、象郡。

translations.literal: He took the lands of the Hundred Yue in the south and made them Guilin and Xiang commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: He conquered the Hundred Yue in the south and made them the commanderies of Guilin and Xiang.

sentences.zh: 百粵之君頫首係頸，委命下吏。

translations.literal: The lords of the Hundred Yue bowed heads, bound necks, and entrusted their lives to lower officials.
translations.idiomatic: The Yue rulers bowed in submission, offered their necks, and placed their fate in Qin officials' hands.

sentences.zh: 乃使蒙恬北築長城而守藩籬，卻匈奴七百餘里，胡人不敢南下而牧馬，士不敢彎弓而報怨。

translations.literal: He then sent Meng Tian north to build the Great Wall as border fence, repelling the Xiongnu over seven hundred li; Hu people did not dare drive horses south, and bowmen did not dare bend bows for revenge.
translations.idiomatic: He sent Meng Tian north to build the Great Wall as frontier barrier, pushing the Xiongnu back more than seven hundred li so the northern tribes dared not pasture south or draw bows in reprisal.

sentences.zh: 於是廢先王之道，焚百家之言，以愚黔首。

translations.literal: Thereupon he abolished the ways of the former kings and burned the sayings of the hundred schools, to make the common people ignorant.
translations.idiomatic: He then abolished the old kingly teachings and burned the writings of the hundred schools to keep the common people ignorant.

sentences.zh: 墮名城，殺豪俊，收天下之兵聚之咸陽，銷鋒鍉鑄以為金人十二，以弱天下之民。

translations.literal: He toppled famed cities, killed the powerful and outstanding, gathered the realm's weapons and concentrated them at Xianyang, melted blades and arrowheads to cast twelve bronze men, thereby weakening the people of the realm.
translations.idiomatic: He dismantled famous walled cities, killed heroic elites, collected weapons from across the empire to Xianyang, and melted them into twelve giant bronze figures to weaken the populace.

sentences.zh: 然後踐華為城，因河為池，據億丈之城，臨不測之川，以為固。

translations.literal: Only then did he tread Mount Hua as wall and use the River as moat, relying on towering city walls and facing unfathomable waters as security.
translations.idiomatic: Then he treated Mount Hua as rampart and the Yellow River as moat, trusting colossal defenses and perilous waters as impregnable security.

sentences.zh: 良將勁弩，守要害之處，信臣精卒，陳利兵而誰何。

translations.literal: Good generals and strong crossbows guarded strategic points; trusted ministers and elite soldiers arrayed sharp weapons and challenged all comers.
translations.idiomatic: Skilled generals with powerful crossbows held the chokepoints, while trusted ministers and picked troops stood ready with sharpened arms to challenge any threat.

sentences.zh: 天下已定，始皇之心，自以為關中之固，金城千里，子孫帝王萬世之業也。

translations.literal: With the realm already settled, the First Emperor thought the strength of Guanzhong and thousand-li metal walls were an imperial enterprise for sons and grandsons for ten thousand generations.
translations.idiomatic: With the realm unified, the First Emperor believed Guanzhong's defenses were an iron citadel that would secure imperial rule for ten thousand generations.

sentences.zh: 始皇既沒，餘威震于殊俗。

translations.literal: After the First Emperor died, his residual awe still shook distant peoples.
translations.idiomatic: Even after his death, his lingering prestige still cowed distant peoples.

sentences.zh: 然而陳涉，甕牖繩樞之子，甿隸之人，遷徙之徒也，材能不及中庸，非有仲尼、墨翟之知，陶朱、猗頓之富。

translations.literal: Yet Chen She was a son of windowed jars and rope-hinge doors, a common laboring man, one of transported convicts. His ability did not reach the ordinary, and he had neither the wisdom of Confucius and Mo Di nor the wealth of Tao Zhu and Yi Dun.
translations.idiomatic: Yet Chen She was from a poor household, a common laborer among relocated convicts, with neither exceptional talent nor the wisdom of Confucius and Mozi, nor the wealth of Tao Zhu and Yi Dun.

sentences.zh: 躡足行伍之間，而免起阡陌之中，帥罷散之卒，將數百之眾，轉而攻秦。

translations.literal: He set foot among rank-and-file troops and rose suddenly from the field paths, leading exhausted scattered soldiers and commanding several hundred men, then turned to attack Qin.
translations.idiomatic: Still, he rose from peasant paths into the ranks, gathered exhausted stragglers into a few hundred men, and turned them against Qin.

sentences.zh: 斬木為兵，揭竿為旗，天下雲合嚮應，贏糧而景從，山東豪俊遂並起而亡秦族矣。

translations.literal: He cut wood for weapons and raised poles for banners. The realm gathered like clouds and responded; people carried grain and followed like shadows. Heroes east of the passes then all rose together and destroyed Qin's line.
translations.idiomatic: With wooden weapons and pole-banners, he drew responses from everywhere: men came like clouds, supplies followed like shadows, and the eastern heroes rose in concert to end Qin's house.

sentences.zh: 且天下非小弱也；

translations.literal: Moreover, the realm was not small and weak.
translations.idiomatic: And the realm Qin ruled was neither small nor weak.

sentences.zh: 雍州之地，殽函之固，自若也。

translations.literal: The land of Yongzhou and the defenses of Xiao and Han were as before.
translations.idiomatic: Yongzhou's territory and the fortifications of Xiao and Han remained intact.

sentences.zh: 陳涉之位，不齒於齊、楚、燕、趙、韓、魏、宋、衛、中山之君；

translations.literal: Chen She's position could not be compared with the rulers of Qi, Chu, Yan, Zhao, Han, Wei, Song, Wey, and Zhongshan.
translations.idiomatic: Chen She's status was nowhere near that of the kings of Qi, Chu, Yan, Zhao, Han, Wei, Song, Wey, and Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 鉏耰棘矜，不敵於鉤戟長鎩；

translations.literal: Hoes and farm tools could not match hooked halberds and long lances.
translations.idiomatic: Hoes and wooden poles could not match halberds and long spears.

sentences.zh: 適戍之眾，不亢於九國之師；

translations.literal: The conscript garrison crowd did not rival the armies of the nine states.
translations.idiomatic: His conscript garrison men were no match for the nine-state coalition armies.

sentences.zh: 深謀遠慮，行軍用兵之道，非及曩時之士也。

translations.literal: In deep planning and far strategy, and methods of marching and warfare, he did not equal the men of former times.
translations.idiomatic: In strategy, planning, and military art, he did not equal the great men of earlier times.

sentences.zh: 然而成敗異變，功業相反，何也？

translations.literal: Yet success and failure changed oppositely and achievements reversed; why?
translations.idiomatic: Yet outcomes reversed completely: failure became success and success became ruin. Why?

sentences.zh: 試使山東之國與陳涉度長絜大，比權量力，不可同年而語矣。

translations.literal: Suppose we had the eastern states compare length and greatness with Chen She, weighing authority and measuring strength; they cannot be spoken of in the same year.
translations.idiomatic: If you compare the eastern states to Chen She in scale, power, and strength, they are not even fit for comparison.

sentences.zh: 然秦以區區之地，致萬乘之權，招八州而朝同列，百有餘年，然后以六合為家，殽函為宮。

translations.literal: Yet Qin, with a tiny patch of land, attained the power of ten-thousand chariots, summoned eight regions to court peers, for over a hundred years, then made the six directions its home and Xiao-Han its palace.
translations.idiomatic: Yet Qin, from a tiny base, seized imperial power, summoned the states of eight regions to court, and for over a century made all under heaven its household and Xiao-Han its inner palace.

sentences.zh: 一夫作難而七廟墮，身死人手，為天下笑者，何也？

translations.literal: When one man raised revolt, the seven ancestral temples collapsed; [Qin] died by others' hands and became the world's laughingstock. Why?
translations.idiomatic: Then one common man rose in rebellion, and Qin's ancestral temples fell; it perished by others' hands and became the world's joke. Why?

sentences.zh: 仁誼不施，而攻守之勢異也。

translations.literal: Because benevolence and righteousness were not practiced, and the conditions of offense and defense had changed.
translations.idiomatic: Because it never practiced humane and righteous rule, and once circumstances shifted from attack to defense, it could no longer stand.

sentences.zh: 周生亦有言，「舜蓋重童子」，項羽又重童子，豈其苗裔邪？

translations.literal: Master Zhou also said, "Shun likely had double pupils." Xiang Yu also had double pupils; could he have been his descendant?
translations.idiomatic: Master Zhou once said, "Shun likely had double pupils." Xiang Yu too had double pupils; could he have been of that line?

sentences.zh: 何其興之暴也！

translations.literal: How violent and sudden was his rise!
translations.idiomatic: How sudden and explosive his rise was.

sentences.zh: 夫秦失其政，陳涉首難，豪桀蜂起，相與並爭，不可勝數。

translations.literal: When Qin lost its governance, Chen She first raised revolt; heroes rose like swarms, contending together beyond count.
translations.idiomatic: When Qin's rule failed, Chen She raised the first revolt, and heroes swarmed up everywhere in numbers beyond count.

sentences.zh: 然羽非有尺寸，乘勢拔起隴畝之中，三年，遂將五諸侯兵滅秦，分裂天下而威海內，封立王侯，政繇羽出，號為「伯王」，位雖不終，近古以來未嘗有也。

translations.literal: Yet Yu had not even an inch [of base], but riding the moment rose from field ridges. In three years he led troops of five lords to destroy Qin, split the realm, and awe the seas within; he enfeoffed and established kings and marquises, and policy issued from Yu. He was titled "Hegemon-King"; though his position did not finish, since near antiquity there had never been such.
translations.idiomatic: Yet Xiang Yu began with no territorial base at all, rose from the fields on momentum, and in three years led the armies of five lords to destroy Qin, partition the world, and overawe the realm; kings and marquises were created by his word alone. Though his rule was short, nothing quite like it had appeared since antiquity.

sentences.zh: 及羽背關懷楚，放逐義帝，而怨王侯畔己，難矣。

translations.literal: When Yu turned his back on the passes and longed for Chu, exiled the Righteous Emperor, and then resented the lords for betraying him, it was difficult indeed.
translations.idiomatic: But once Xiang Yu turned away from Guanzhong for Chu, exiled the Righteous Emperor, and then blamed the feudal kings for deserting him, his position became untenable.

sentences.zh: 自矜功伐，奮其私智而不師古，始霸王之國，欲以力征經營天下，五年卒亡其國，身死東城，尚不覺寤，不自責過失，乃引「天亡我，非用兵之罪」，豈不謬哉！

translations.literal: Proud of his own merit, exerting private cleverness without taking antiquity as teacher, he had begun a hegemon-king's state and wished by force to conquer and manage the realm; in five years he finally lost his state and died at Dongcheng, yet still did not awaken, did not blame his own faults, and instead said, "Heaven destroys me; it is not the fault of military use" - is this not absurd!
translations.idiomatic: He gloried in his achievements, trusted his private cleverness, and refused historical example. He sought to rule all by force; within five years his state was ruined and he died at Dongcheng, yet even then he never recognized his own errors, claiming only, "Heaven destroyed me, not military fault." How could that not be delusion?
